source_metadata.title_raw: Joel Fajardo - A-19-015 - March 15, 2019 - San Fernando

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 15, 2019


Mayor Joel Fajardo
229 N Meyer St
San Fernando, CA 91340

Re:	Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-015

Dear Mr. Fajardo:

	This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

	Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

	Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

	Do you have a conflict of interest in the City's Cannabis Ordinance decision due to your residence's proximity to the San Fernando Corridors Specific Plan ("SP-5") zone?

CONCLUSION

	No. The decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on your residential property.



___________________
	¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-015
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The City of San Fernando ("City") is a general law city located in the County of Los
Angeles. The City is approximately 2.37 square miles in area, and is bordered by the City of Los
Angeles on all sides. Except for allowing deliveries of medicinal cannabis products from licensed
businesses located outside of the City's boundaries, all other adult-use and medicinal commercial
cannabis activities are currently prohibited in all zones throughout the City.

A. The City's Proposed Cannabis Ordinance

The City Council will soon consider the adoption of a proposed ordinance to regulate
commercial cannabis cultivation, distribution, manufacturing, and laboratory testing within certain
specified City zones, including the City's San Fernando Corridors Specific Plan ("SP-5") zone.²
The Cannabis Ordinance would establish regulations and a discretionary review process to allow
the following:

• Commercial cannabis cultivation, distribution, and manufacturing in the M-1 (Limited
Industrial) and M-2 (Light Industrial) manufacturing zones; and the Workplace Flex District
within the SP-5 zone.

• Commercial cannabis laboratory testing in the C-1 (Limited Commercial), C-2
(Commercial), and SC (Service Commercial) zones, including the M-1
(Limited Industrial) and M-2 (Light Industrial) manufacturing zones; and the Workplace
Flex, Mixed-Use Corridor and Auto Commercial Districts within the SP-5 zone.³

• Provide a 450-foot buffer between cannabis businesses and sensitive receptors (i.e., day
cares, youth centers, and K-12 public and private schools).

The SP-5 zone incorporated under the Cannabis Ordinance is comprised of 224 parcels. As
described above, the proposed Cannabis Ordinance would allow medicinal and adult use
commercial cannabis cultivation, distribution, manufacturing, and laboratory testing in the
Workplace Flex, Mixed-Use Corridor and Auto Commercial Districts within the SP-5 zone. The
proposed Ordinance allows for indoor cultivation only, and includes "operational standards that
would regulate noise, odors and minimize impacts to surrounding uses."⁴ The Ordinance would
prohibit commercial cannabis retail (dispensaries and deliveries) and microbusinesses citywide
except permitted medicinal cannabis delivery activities originating from outside of the City
boundaries. The proposed ordinance would also not allow cannabis-related business activities in the
General Neighborhood District.

² The City's SP-5 zone is made up of sub-districts, each of which have different development restrictions and
parameters (e.g., Downtown, Mixed-Use Corridor, Workplace Flex, General Neighborhood, Maclay District and Auto
Commercial).

³ Allowance within the Workplace Flex, Mixed-Use Corridor and Auto Commercial Districts within the SP-5
zone would be consistent with where current SP-5 regulations conditionally permit non-cannabis laboratories.

⁴ See the City Planning Commission Resolution no. 2018-009, http://ci.san-fernando.ca.us/wp-
content/uploads/2018/12/PP-Comm-Reso-No-2018-009-11-7-18.pdf as viewed March 13, 2019.

File No. A-19-015
Page No. 3

You do not live within the SP-5 zone. You own and reside in a single-family home, located
within 500 feet of the SP-5 zone area, General Neighborhood District. Your neighborhood consists
of single-family residences. Your residence is 615 feet from the Workplace Flex District; 1,060 feet
from the Mixed-Use Corridor District; and 4,540 feet from the Auto Commercial District in the SP-
5 zone.

This decision will not involve any approvals for specific projects. It merely establishes
regulations for commercial cannabis cultivation, distribution, manufacturing, and laboratory testing
within these locations. Any new cannabis business will require separate individualized review and
discretionary approval based on its location within the specified zones designated under the
Cannabis Ordinance and the particular features of the project.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of
the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The financial interest
implicated by these facts is your real property interest in your residence. (Section 87103(b).)

Foreseeability and Materiality

The standard for foreseeability varies depending on whether an interest is explicitly
involved in the decision at issue. A financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable where the
official's financial interest is explicitly involved as a named party in, or subject of, the decision.⁵
(Regulation 18701(a).) For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, the
financial effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation
18701(b).) Your residence is not explicitly involved in this decision, so the latter foreseeability
standard applies.

Real Property

Regulation 18702.2 provides the materiality standard for real property interests, which
varies depending on the proximity of the decision's impacts to your property interest.⁶ Although
your home is located within 500 feet of the SP-5 zone, General Neighborhood District, the
decision at issue does not pertain to the SP-5 zone area as a whole, and is limited to certain
districts within the SP-5 zone. More specifically, the decision may allow commercial cannabis

⁵ A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, or if the
decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).

⁶ Regulation 18702.2 was amended by the Commission on January 17, 2019 and is effective at the end of
March, 2019. We apply the amended version of this regulation for this analysis.

File No. A-19-015
Page No. 4

cultivation, distribution, and manufacturing activity as well as laboratory testing in the Workplace
Flex District, located 615 feet from your residence. This is an industrial, warehouse area that
already permits other commercial laboratory use.⁷

Regulation 18702.2(a)(8) states materiality is found where the decision involves property
more than 500 feet, but less than 1,000 feet from the official's parcel, and would change the
development, income producing potential, or highest and best use of the official's parcel; or if it
would change the character of the official's parcel by substantially altering traffic levels, intensity
of use, parking, views, privacy, noise levels, or air quality. There is no indication that permitting
cannabis commercial business activity within the Workplace Flex District under the proposed
Ordinance would impact your residence or residential neighborhood differently than the existing
permitted types of business uses.

The Ordinance would also allow this type of business use in the Mixed-Use Corridor
District and the Auto Commercial District, each located over 1,000 feet from your residence.
Regulation 18702.2(b) states:

(b) The financial effect of a governmental decision on a parcel of real property in
which an official has a financial interest involving property 1,000 feet or more
from the property line of the official's property is presumed not to be material.
This presumption may be rebutted with clear and convincing evidence the
governmental decision would have a substantial effect on the official's property.

With regard to the Districts located over 1,000 feet from your residential property, there is
no indication that this decision will have any effect on your residential property. Therefore, you do
not have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial interest in this decision.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

___________________________
⁷ http://ci.san-fernando.ca.us/wp-content/uploads/2018/01/171220_SFCSP_Final_Rev_02.pdf, as viewed
February, 27, 2019.

Do you have a conflict of interest in the City's Cannabis Ordinance decision due to your

your residential property.

QUESTION: Do you have a conflict of interest in the City's Cannabis Ordinance decision due to your 
residence's proximity to the San Fernando Corridors Specific Plan ("SP-5") zone?

CONCLUSION: No. The decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on 
your residential property.
